Module 5 -Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 5
Speaker 1: All right, guys. Welcome back. This is Module 5. By this point in the course, you've
already brought your prospects onto the journey, and you've really challenged their
beliefs. Under your leadership, together you've lovingly dissected and disassembled
many of those major reasons why they haven't achieved the outcomes they're looking
for. In doing so, you're really built trust. You've accurately reflected back their world,
their context, and offered a more encouraging, and even more importantly, a fixable
diagnosis. Now with all that said, it's time to prescribe. The launch as we're using it in
this course is that initial moment where you're product is now available for sale.
Whereas after a few days or as much as 10 days in some cases of building anticipation in
your prelaunch and coaching them to the point where your product will be a natural fit
into their new context and new reality with all the shifted beliefs and reframes, it's now
time to make that introduction.
I like to call this the tipping point. If you look at our process map here, you'll see that the
launch is where it starts to hit that downward arc. We start using the forces of gravity
that we've built up. That force really comes from the new context, the new beliefs, the
new attitudes, the new feelings they have about their problem about you, about what
they're trying to achieve. This is where we have that downward momentum. It's a lot
more difficult to build up new beliefs, to have them release a lot of their old attitudes.
That's why we do it on that first part and that path of less friction. There's friction once
there's a product. That's why we do all that groundwork in that place of lesser friction.
At this point, now we introduce that product where it could be this downward slope.
We want to really capitalize on that momentum and bring your prospect across that
buying line with a powerful launch sequence.
The way I like to equate the launch, I keep this image in front of me actually on my
desktop when I'm writing the launch day emails. It's just these waves. Waves are
peaceful, they're natural, but at the same time they're a very powerful force. At this
point, we want to ride the momentum of the current. We want our prospect to ride the
momentum of that current because you really can't swim against it. There is this sense
of ease and naturalness to it in allowing those waves to just carry you to that shore, but
at the same time swimming against it could be this very violent, and even dangerous,
and just not a good situation. We really want to capitalize on that and give them the
chance to ride that momentum in a very natural way instead of resisting it. Even though
this is a really short sequence, either one or two emails, there's actually quite a lot we
need to be aware of and quite a few mistakes that marketers make that not only
sabotage sales at the beginning of the launch but also make it much more difficult to sell
and convert later as well. We're going to dive into a complete breakdown of exactly
what we're coaching here.
First and foremost, what this sequence is coaching is progress and sustained
momentum. This is exactly what I was just alluding to. The goal is to really reflect back
to them how far they've come, how far they've traveled, and how the cost of going
backwards would really be going against the grain. There's more resistance in that.
There's more risk in that. We're really actually creating a sense of loss aversion around
momentum itself. We talk so much about loss aversion around tangible items, but like
we reference in Module 3, loss aversion could also be with your exclusive
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 5 --

Module 5 -Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 5
empowerment privilege points. No one wants to lose out on that chance to capitalize on
it.
The same applies to the momentum they've generated. No one wants to lose their
momentum. The way we do this is by also selling your prospect on the fact that Rome is
still burning. If for a second you imagine that the journey we're taking them on from this
locked door within a burning building, and we unlocked it for them and then showed
them a new path, and helped clear the smoke, and sheltered them from those flames,
and we finally brought them to this fire escape. All they have to do is step out, take one
step instead of going back into that burning building. That is what we want to create
here.
Where marketers get this terribly wrong is that they mistakenly try to create urgency
here by dropping a time deadline. Some people say, "Only open for five days." Ironically,
the only thing that that's selling is procrastination, a sense that we can wait, that the
flames we were running from and trying to escape aren't as threatening as we thought,
that after all that running and climbing, we can afford now to just take a break, rest our
feet, chill out and take a few breaths. When we try to use time urgency too early,
anywhere before the 48-hour mark really, what we're really doing is putting out the fire
for them and giving them a reason to procrastinate, dowsing the flames of desire and
urgency by giving them permission to chill.
What we really want to do is communicate that Rome is still burning. Instead, that's
really what we want to do. That's what keeping their best interest in mind will really
dictate here. We have to warn them about the perils of going back, telling them that
there's really nothing to go back to, that the old reality, the one they existed in before
all these reframes and new beliefs we've inserted is a burning inferno that's about to
crumble to the ground at any second. There's really only an illusion of going backwards.
It sound dramatic, and obviously your wording of this will be very different of whether
or not you're selling something that's literally life or death or less and inherently urgent
like language skills, for example. If you're not willing to ring that fire alarm, how do you
expect your prospect to? Your job is still to find the life or death moment of that
situation, that moment of highest tension that we spoke about before. There's always
going to be a burning fire in any market. Your job is to communicate that, communicate
the urgency of that and have them take that simple step out of it.
The other thing we want to say is that Rome is still burning, but it's been burning for a
while. A second element is that the fire didn't just start the moment you showed up on
the scene with your product launch or your funnel. You actually want to show them that
they've been trying to solve this problem or achieve this outcome for a long time. By
extending the timeline previous to their encounter with you, you get them to notice
that their desire is genuine. It's not something manufactured just within the scope of
your launch. What we're going to be doing here is we're extending the consistency
principle into the indefinite past, showing them how they've committed to that result
forever. Now with your help, they're on the cusp of it. All roads have led to this
moment, which really amplifies a sense of completion, vindication, and redemption,
which is that culmination of the buyer's journey, if we could get it here.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 5 --

Module 5 -Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 3 of 5
It's that Andy Dufresne moment again, the ending of Shawshank. It doesn't have to be
as climactic as being in the rain and arms flailing and all that, but it's powerful. It's
powerful. Why was that ending in Shawshank so powerful? Andy Dufresne spent over a
decade in jail for a crime he didn't commit. It's over an injustice. The greater the
injustice, the more redeeming it's going to be. That's really the sense of completion we
want to give your prospect. In the prelaunch, we've absolved them of guilt. That makes
them innocent, innocent to the pain and struggle which becomes that injustice. The
market, your competitors, the sacred cows you've slaughtered in the prelaunch have
become the oppressors, the warden. Then by showing them that the time they've been
serving has extended well beyond when you came along, that's when we get that
amplified sense of vindication. That's what we're communicating here. Rome is burning.
Rome has been burning for a while. Maybe it's been burning forever.
With all that said, if they can't go back into the burning inferno, or the jail cell, or
whatever metaphor you want to use and can only go foward into safety, into that new
future, into that new step, it's your job to frame it that way. It's your job to actually
make that step as natural but necessary as possible. Make that step out of the fire as
easy as possible. I like to frame this, who's more willing to escape the fire, the person
who just has to take a small step onto flat land, or the person who has to jump out of a
second story window? It's your job to give them that easy, soft cushion to land on. Think
about that tipping point I showed you a few minutes ago. Our prelaunch already took
them through the tough part, the confrontational breathing through the fire and
escaping those flames. They made it, and now they just need to claim the prize, claim
the rewards of that journey.
We strategically made them take the most confronting and challenging part of the
journey during that time of least friction without the offer. Now that there is friction in
taking out a credit card, we leverage the same principle by offering both a small step
and inviting them to ride that momentum. You could think of a car that hits friction. It's
like driving through thick snow. You just have a much better chance to get through it if
you're using momentum and speed than if you stop. When we get to the workbook and
the theme plates, I'm going to walk you through a really effective and simple way of
doing this, to get buy-in and commitment to that next step actually right before you
open the fire escape and ask them to step out.
Another thing you're coaching here is you're coaching against procrastination. There are
a lot of benefits in getting people to buy right away when you open your cart. First and
foremost, you can get cashflow to reinvest in ads and retargeting. Isn't it a lot nicer to
just get money in right away? That's one thing we want to do. It also gives you
confidence, confidence that people actually want your product, that it's validated, that
it's working, that your launch is actually succeeding, your marketing is actually working.
These are very important signs, especially mid-launch. It just gives you more confidence
to continue marketing to maybe reinvest in those ads, and it also gives your partners
confidence if you have affiliates and partners mailing out for you to show that you're
converting. That's a big deal. That can make a big, big difference in the long run if they
see right off the bat that, wow, this is really working. People really want it. It definitely
pays to get a lot of sales right off the bat. Sure, we are still going to see the bulk of our
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 5 --

Module 5 -Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 4 of 5
sales in those closing hours. That's not going to change. If we could just get momentum
right out of the gates, that's really going to be a big deal for us.
Another thing we're doing is we're setting up the sale for later. One way to do this,
which you'll see in the theme plates, is in creating a fast action bonus. This actually
works on two levels because on one hand you're encouraging people who are ready to
buy to buy, but on another level it gives you a chance to not just tell but show your
other prospects the tangible cost of inaction. For those that miss out on a fast action
bonus at the beginning, they'll see right away that there's a real cost in waiting. It starts
to dissuade inaction. They really get to see what's going on here.
By giving your prospects a taste of FOMO in a cart close light scenario with that fast
action bonus, they actually get acquainted with the pain of missing out. It's still fresh
enough in their mind so that a few days later when you're going to pull the plug again,
they're less likely to miss out. The lesson is still fresh in their mind. It's like their hand is
still burning from the last time they touched the stove. They just didn't like that feeling.
It's a cart close light. Consider it really a dress rehearsal for your final close so that by
the time you get to the end of your sequence, they're just already familiar with the pain
of missing out, and they know what to do to avoid it this time.
This is what really coaches action later on. How many of you have missed an
opportunity, felt the FOMO, and then vowed to never let that happen again? That's
what we're trying to achieve. Yes, it's going to bring more buyers in right away, but it's
also to sell them against procrastination later in the sequence. This is also just the right
way to add legitimate urgency at this stage. I mentioned earlier how the biggest mistake
is trying to create urgency around a three or five-day cart close deadline, which actually
has the counter effect. Having a fast action bonus is where you get to leverage actual
urgency while adding an extra incentive to ride that momentum through the right way.
What types of fast action bonuses might you consider here? There are three types that I
would usually work with here. One is something that can help them achieve the
outcome quicker. It has to have real world value. I think non-scalable really works here.
If there's any spots that leverages that involves your one on one time or the time of
your team, those are things that work really well as a quick action bonus because it's
authentic scarcity. There is a real hard limit on that. That is something that is valuable.
People do value your time. Just having that non-scalable aspect to it makes the scarcity
there feel real, makes the urgency around it feel real instead of forced. That's what I'd
like to use there. That's usually what I recommend. It could even be a ticket to a limited
capacity live event. If you're hosting an intimate gathering for people in your program,
you could do that. Or once again, just access to your team's time, coaching, group
coaching, quick start implementation sessions, all that works really well at this stage.
Finally, the last thing I really want to mention before we get into the workbook and the
theme plates is this. Many of us have been trained to build hype and treat our launch as
this big deal. We have a grand reveal and start talking about and only about how maybe
it's been a labor of love for eons, how our pets now hate us, how this is our life's
magnum opus. It's all about us. By all means, be proud of what went into it and what
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 5 --

Module 5 -Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 5 of 5
you sacrificed to do it, and inspire trust in your audience by letting them know that what
they're getting is really a distillation of years, and years, and years of research, work,
experience, and investment. But just remember, the big deal isn't the fact that you've
created this thing. It's that they're now able to use it to vindicate and free themselves.
I'm going to say that one more time because it's really the most important takeaway.
The big deal isn't the fact that you spent all this time, all these years crafting the perfect
program and pouring your heart, soul, blood, tears, fluids, whatever into this. It's really
that they are now able to take it and use it to vindicate themselves and free themselves
from all the past previous attempts around solving this problem. That's the big deal. I
really want you to internalize that when writing this launch email because it's so
tempting to make it about us and our process because I know how hard you worked to
create your offer. I know how much went into it. I know you want your audience to
know about all that. I'm not saying avoid that completely. I'm just saying it's all about
where you put the focus. The big deal is what they are now able to achieve, the
outcomes they're able to now realize with your help.
On that note, I'm going to jump out and I'm going to meet you back in the workbook in
just a second where we're going to break down some questions and some theme plates
to help you execute this really important mini-sequence within your launch. I'll see you
there in a bit.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
